event: Copper Age settlements such as those at Wari-Bateshwar develop in the Bengal delta during the Chalcolithic period.
The fortified urban site of Wari-Bateshwar flourishes as an early trading center engaged in regional commerce.
The territory becomes part of the Mauryan Empire, with Pundranagara (modern Mahasthangarh) serving as a major administrative city.
Buddhism spreads through the region under Mauryan influence following Emperor Ashoka's patronage.
The Gupta Empire incorporates Bengal, ushering in a classical era of art, learning, and Sanskrit culture.
King Shashanka establishes the Gauda Kingdom, regarded as the first independent unified polity in the Bengal region.
Gopala is elected as the first Pala king, founding the Buddhist Pala Empire that dominates Bengal for centuries.
Emperor Dharmapala expands Pala power across northern India and patronizes the great monastery of Somapura Mahavihara.
The Kaivarta revolt under Divya temporarily challenges Pala rule in northern Bengal.
The Hindu Sena dynasty rises to rule Bengal, displacing the declining Pala Empire.
Muhammad bin Bakhtiyar Khalji conquers Bengal for the Delhi Sultanate, beginning centuries of Muslim rule.
Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah founds the independent Bengal Sultanate with its capital at Pandua.
Ilyas Shah unifies Bengal, fostering a distinct Bengali Muslim cultural and linguistic identity.
The Mughal Empire defeats the Bengal Sultanate at the Battle of Rajmahal, annexing the region.
Dhaka is established as the Mughal provincial capital of Bengal and named Jahangirnagar.
Murshid Quli Khan establishes the largely autonomous Nawabs of Bengal under nominal Mughal authority.
The British East India Company defeats Nawab Siraj-ud-Daulah at the Battle of Plassey, beginning British dominance over Bengal.
Victory at the Battle of Buxar secures Company control over the revenues of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa.
The Great Bengal Famine kills an estimated ten million people, roughly a third of the population, under Company misrule.
The Permanent Settlement institutes the zamindari landholding system across Bengal.
The Indian Rebellion spreads to Bengal, after which the British Crown assumes direct rule over India.
The British partition of Bengal creates a separate eastern province, sparking widespread nationalist protest.
The partition of Bengal is annulled amid sustained agitation, reuniting the province.
The Bengal Famine causes an estimated two to three million deaths during the Second World War.
The Partition of India creates East Bengal as the eastern wing of the new state of Pakistan.
Police kill student protesters in Dhaka during the Bengali Language Movement, later commemorated as International Mother Language Day.
East Bengal is renamed East Pakistan under the One Unit administrative scheme.
Sheikh Mujibur Rahman proclaims the Six Point Movement demanding greater autonomy for East Pakistan.
The Bhola cyclone kills hundreds of thousands, and the Awami League wins a landslide in Pakistan's general election.
Bangladesh declares independence and wins the Liberation War after a genocidal crackdown, supported by Indian intervention against Pakistan.
Sheikh Mujibur Rahman becomes the first leader of independent Bangladesh and a new constitution is adopted.
Bangladesh is admitted to the United Nations as a member state and suffers a devastating famine.
Sheikh Mujibur Rahman and most of his family are assassinated in a military coup, triggering years of political instability.
General Ziaur Rahman assumes the presidency and reorients national policy after consolidating military-backed power.
President Ziaur Rahman is assassinated in an attempted military coup in Chittagong.
General Hussain Muhammad Ershad seizes power in a bloodless coup and establishes military rule.
Islam is declared the state religion of Bangladesh through a constitutional amendment.
A mass pro-democracy uprising forces President Ershad to resign, restoring parliamentary democracy.
Khaleda Zia becomes the country's first female prime minister, and a catastrophic cyclone kills around 138,000 people.
Sheikh Hasina leads the Awami League to power as prime minister for the first time.
Muhammad Yunus and the Grameen Bank win the Nobel Peace Prize for pioneering microfinance.
A military-backed caretaker government declares a state of emergency amid political crisis.
Sheikh Hasina returns as prime minister after an Awami League election victory, beginning a long tenure in power.
The Rana Plaza garment factory collapse kills over 1,100 workers, prompting global scrutiny of the textile industry.
Hundreds of thousands of Rohingya refugees flee Myanmar into Bangladesh, creating vast camps at Cox's Bazar.
The Padma Bridge, the country's largest infrastructure project, opens to connect the southwest to the capital.
A student-led mass uprising forces Sheikh Hasina to resign and flee, ending her fifteen-year rule and bringing an interim government led by Muhammad Yunus.
